title: Anthroponymy

(also **anthroponymics** or **anthroponomastics**, from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") ἄνθρωπος *anthrōpos*, 'human', and ὄνομα *onoma*, 'name') is the study of *anthroponyms*, the [proper names](/wiki/Proper_names "Proper names") of [human beings](/wiki/Human_beings "Human beings"), both individual and collective.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom19968-1) Anthroponymy is a branch of [onomastics](/wiki/Onomastics "Onomastics").

**Anthroponymy** (also **anthroponymics** or **anthroponomastics**, from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") ἄνθρωπος *anthrōpos*, 'human', and ὄνομα *onoma*, 'name') is the study of *anthroponyms*, the [proper names](/wiki/Proper_names "Proper names") of [human beings](/wiki/Human_beings "Human beings"), both individual and collective.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom19968-1) Anthroponymy is a branch of [onomastics](/wiki/Onomastics "Onomastics").

Researchers in the field of anthroponymy are called *anthroponymists*. Since the study of anthroponyms is relevant for several other disciplines within [social sciences](/wiki/Social_sciences "Social sciences") and [humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), experts from those disciplines engage in anthroponymic studies, including researchers from the fields of [anthropology](/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology"),[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBruckBodenhorn2009-2) [history](/wiki/History "History"),[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiolkowska2011383–398-3) [human geography](/wiki/Human_geography "Human geography"),[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBourinMartínez_Sopena2010-4) [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology"), [prosopography](/wiki/Prosopography "Prosopography"),[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFossier201034-5) and [genealogy](/wiki/Genealogy "Genealogy").

Anthroponymists follow certain principles, rules and criteria when researching anthroponyms. The methods used for research are divided into two major categories: the collecting of anthroponymic information and the analysis and interpretation of anthroponyms. The collection of anthroponymic information includes: inscriptions, documents, [onomastics](/wiki/Onomastics "Onomastics")-tax records, dictionaries, phone books, [monographs](/wiki/Monograph "Monograph"), and websites, which are used afterward for mapping purposes. The analysis and interpretation of anthroponyms take into account the processing of the collection of the information gathered, which consists of [linguistic analysis](/wiki/Linguistic_description "Linguistic description"), comparative-historical method, [geographical](/wiki/Geographical "Geographical") method, and [statistical](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") method.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Anthroponymy of individual and family names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthroponymy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Anthroponymy of individual and family names")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Cartouches_of_Ramesses_III.jpg/250px-Cartouches_of_Ramesses_III.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cartouches_of_Ramesses_III.jpg)

Name of pharaoh [Ramesses III](/wiki/Ramesses_III "Ramesses III"), written in [hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs")

Anthroponymy of individual and family names, and their mutual correlations, includes the study of:

* [Personal names](/wiki/Personal_name "Personal name")
* [Given names](/wiki/Given_name "Given name")
* [Surnames](/wiki/Surname "Surname")
* [Nicknames](/wiki/Nickname "Nickname")
* [Pseudonyms](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym")
* [Mononyms](/wiki/Mononymous_person "Mononymous person")
* [Matronyms](/wiki/Matronym "Matronym")
* [Patronyms](/wiki/Patronym "Patronym")
* [Eponyms](/wiki/Eponym "Eponym")
* [Teknonyms](/wiki/Teknonymy "Teknonymy")

Anthroponyms of individuals can also be classified according to [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender"). Names of human males are called *andronyms* (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") ἀνήρ / man, and ὄνομα / name),[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom19966-7) while names of human females are called *gynonyms* (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") γυνή / woman, and ὄνομα / name).[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarolini200591,_98-8)

## Anthroponymy of group and population names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthroponymy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Anthroponymy of group and population names")]

Anthroponymy of group and population names includes the study of [demonyms](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") (names of localized populations),[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2017205-220-9) [ethnonyms](/wiki/Ethnonym "Ethnonym") (names of ethnic groups),[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom199638-39-10) as well as [tribal names](/wiki/Tribal_name "Tribal name") and [clan names](/wiki/Clan "Clan").

## Anthroponymy and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthroponymy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Anthroponymy and culture")]

Anthroponymy is a socio-cultural tool that can be used to find out about an individual's [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture"). Through the name of a person, their [nationality](/wiki/Nationality "Nationality"), as well as their [history](/wiki/History "History"), can be traced. Anthroponyms have both a national and cultural significance as they guarantee the preservation of [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), [cultural](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), and [historical](/wiki/History "History") information.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Related terms and processes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anthroponymy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Related terms and processes")]

There are several specific terms and processes related to anthroponymy, like:

* *anthroponymization*, a process when an anthroponym is formed from an [apellative](/wiki/Apellative "Apellative"), like when a surname is created from the name of ones occupation, thus forming an [occupational surname](/wiki/Occupational_surname "Occupational surname"). Such surnames are common in most languages, including English: *[Smith](/wiki/Smith_(surname) "Smith (surname)")* (from [smith](/wiki/Metalsmith "Metalsmith")), *[Miller](/wiki/Miller_(name) "Miller (name)")* (from [miller](/wiki/Miller "Miller")), *[Thatcher](/wiki/Thatcher_(disambiguation)#Surname "Thatcher (disambiguation)")* (from [thatcher](/wiki/Thatcher_(profession) "Thatcher (profession)")), *[Shepherd](/wiki/Shepherd_(surname) "Shepherd (surname)")* (from [shepherd](/wiki/Shepherd "Shepherd")), or *[Potter](/wiki/Potter_(surname) "Potter (surname)")* (from [potter](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery")).[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom19969-11)
* *deanthroponymization*, a process when an anthroponym becomes an [apellative](/wiki/Apellative "Apellative"), like when the surname of the inventor [Louis Braille](/wiki/Louis_Braille "Louis Braille") was used to create a name for the writing system for visually impaired persons ([braille](/wiki/Braille "Braille")).[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom199628-12)
* *transonymization* of anthroponyms into [toponyms](/wiki/Toponyms "Toponyms"), a process when a human proper name is used to form a [toponym](/wiki/Toponym "Toponym") (proper name of a locality; place name), thus creating an *[anthropotoponym](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/anthropotoponym "wikt:anthropotoponym")*, like when the name of [Alexander the Great](/wiki/Alexander_the_Great "Alexander the Great") was used to create several *astionyms* (city names), including for the newly created city of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") in ancient [Hellenistic Egypt](/wiki/Hellenistic_Egypt "Hellenistic Egypt"), or when the surname of [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") was used to create several *choronyms* (region names), including names for the South American state of [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), and the Canadian province of [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoom199630-13)
* *transonymization* of toponyms into anthroponyms, a process when toponyms (place names) are used to form human names (anthroponyms), thus creating various *topoanthroponyms*.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Many [surnames](/wiki/Surnames "Surnames") are created in this way, and they are known as [toponymic surnames](/wiki/Toponymic_surnames "Toponymic surnames"). Most [demonyms](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") (names for localized populations) are *topoanthroponyms* by formation, since they are usually created from toponyms, and also some [ethnonyms](/wiki/Ethnonym "Ethnonym") are *topoanthroponyms* too (those that are formed from toponyms, and thus referred to as *topoethnonyms*).[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReis201358–61-15) For example, geographic designations for the region of *Black Mountain* ([Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro")) and frontier region of *Ukraina* ([Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine")) were used to create not only demonyms for general populations for those regions, but also ethnonyms for modern [ethnic Montenegrins](/wiki/Ethnic_Montenegrins "Ethnic Montenegrins") and [ethnic Ukrainians](/wiki/Ethnic_Ukrainians "Ethnic Ukrainians").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDanver2015348-349,_384-387-16)
